speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews out from the Polish from that woman. That woman, before I left, I said look-- if I survive but I couldn't write to her, and she thought that I'm not alive-- if I survive, I told her the real name of my daughter, the name it was before. And I said-- I gave her my photo. I had still somewhere hidden my photo. And I said, if I don't survive, keep her. Because I didn't believe that anybody else would survive. I thought that there would not be left one Jewish soul. Everybody thought so. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, keep her and convert her to Christianity only-- said, she'll be alive. She kept her, and she wanted to have from her a maid to help her, to clean, you know, just like a, a, a maid at home. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That woman-- after the war, there were a few people like that, like you people, volunteers helped. They helped. They knew that there were Jewish people hidden in non-Jewish homes, in convents. A lot were in convents. And people-- parents gave the children to a convent here and there. And she knew about it. The woman who-- whom I-- who told me the first news that she's alive. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She and other people worked tirelessly going around to the wharf, finding out where in convents, where Jewish children were, to get them out. Because they knew otherwise if they were left there, they'll never be Jewish-- Jewish. They'll be brought up as Christians. I know a few children who were in convents. Even France, they got out the children. She went from house to house looking for Jewish children, just openly asking for money, money they get-- got from the United States. A lot of money-- United States-- how do you call it-- like [NON-ENGLISH]-- sent in money to get out-- they-- and you ask them did they need money to pay some Polish people. They pay them off, they can get out. Otherwise they had the children. They didn't want to give them back, not because they loved them so much, but to convert them to Christianity. That woman, she was a rabbi's wife, the one who told me my husband's alive in Nuremberg. She and others, some others, went around from house to house, from convent to convent, from church to church, looking for children and paying a lot of money-- they got a lot of money from Jewish com-- from United States-- Jewish-- how do they call them-- like [NON-ENGLISH]-- to pay to get out the children. As they went on Grudetska Street, where that woman lived, side of Grudetska, they saw a little girl-- winter on snow, barefoot, only in a little shirt or nightdress like this, shivering, with no hair, as I have the photo there. I have it-- that's-- she was already [INAUDIBLE]-- on the street. They ran after her, out of the house where she was. That was my daughter. They ran after her, two women. And she was there. She told me this story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, little girl-- and they had with them with them, in a bag, dolls and everything to tempt them out-- many were left in convents and [INAUDIBLE]-- and said, little girl, what's your name? [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: She understood already. It was after the war. [NON-ENGLISH] I don't want Jews. Jews will kill me. She taught her all the time, don't go to Jews when I'm not at home, because they will kill you. They didn't want to give her back. [NON-ENGLISH], Jews will kill me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, when they heard that, they knew it. They knew now it's a Jewish girl. It's a Jewish child. Your mommy was Jewish, wasn't she? No, no, no, no, no my mommy. No. She was afraid because she taught her all the time, don't talk to anyone. Don't tell anyone that you are Jewish. No. And ran back into that house.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, they didn't do anything more, two women. And one was the one that had [INAUDIBLE] with me in nursing school. And she was skilled. As she ran in, they ran after her looking where she goes in. They've seen exactly where she went in. She didn't see them behind her. That was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this-- no, I think the Soviets were there still, because after the war when the Germans went back, the Soviets came back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went-- they wouldn't do it, but they got a Soviet soldier, and they told him so, look. Here is a [INAUDIBLE] child. They knew about it. We'll pay you how much you want. Come visit, horses-- and how do you call it-- draft horses and get her out somehow. Go to that woman. She keeps a Jewish child, a [INAUDIBLE] child.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, he wanted the money. He took his sack-- you know, a sack. [NON-ENGLISH]. And she was wait-- waiting outside. And he went into the house without knocking, went in and said he wanted to scare that woman [INAUDIBLE] children and said, yeah, yeah, [NON-ENGLISH] to scare them. [NON-ENGLISH] means, in Russian, a Jewish child. She said no. But she's very inquisitive, my daughter, and always very intelligent. She was-- it was at night time-- in a bed together with-- there were two children together-- a bed that was not a bed. It was on the ground in racks. And as she heard [INAUDIBLE], she got up her head and listened. She said, what is it. The others lied there, and they didn't-- at all. They were not interested. But she got--

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went up with the sack, put her into a sack like a little animal over his back and ran out. As he ran out, she started scream, I'll go to the Jews and I'll tell them that the mother told me that-- then said yes-- the mother told me that I can keep her if she's killed. And the mother is killed. That was much before. And I want-- and took stones and threw the stones at--

speaker: interviewee
text: But he said, all right. I am the police. If you do it again, we'll put you into prison. It wasn't true at all. And that woman was a Jewish woman, you know, that he-- I met later. Straightaway he said, I'll take you to my place. She paid him, she was in his place. And she started to scream, I want to go back to my [NON-ENGLISH]. I want to my-- I don't want Jews. You'll kill me.

speaker: interviewer
text: She told me later. This is the whole story. And I asked her, is it true, [INAUDIBLE]? And she said, yes. I was told like this, and I didn't know anything else. I know that the Jews killed. I said, but the Germans killed, not the Jews. She told me the Jews, and I hated the Jews. I had to battle with her a long time later to convince her that it's not so at all. She told her because she wanted to keep her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she went to the Jewish community and told them [INAUDIBLE]. Said you can't help it. Have the money. If you don't want money, you'll get nothing. She took the money later and said how they got out and got it to her. And later, you know, the orphanage and other places. And that's how the story went with her. And if she goes back, she doesn't want to think of it. She doesn't-- she would-- you know, there is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the young survivors, were-- you know, were-- she's not interested at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war finished, did you think about going back to Grodek?

speaker: interviewee
text: About whom?

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war finished, did you think about going back to your hometown, Grodek?

speaker: interviewee
text: To go back where?

speaker: interviewer
text: To your hometown.

speaker: interviewee
text: That woman, I think sometimes, I wouldn't-- I wouldn't-- you see, that German woman, that was different. She saved me. She saved me and paid for me. That woman, I wouldn't do anything to help her because she starved her. Right, she kept her. She kept her. But when she went out barefoot in winter on the street, she never thought of anything. And not only that, she told me a story. She told me that that woman kept-- I don't know-- they haven't got it here-- like triangles, sugar, like stones, sugar. They-- that was sugar. And they kept them from year to year. She had it somewhere on a-- high on a like, on a wardrobe on top, you know, like here, high up. She was very little. She couldn't reach it. She didn't eat for days. She did-- she went-- she took her children to the market where she saw bread. I told you this story, that woman-- and fed her children with I don't know, whatever she bought-- a piece of meat and bread and they ate. So they didn't eat much. And they were both well fed, while mine was-- she never brought her a little piece of bread from-- that she gave her children. She never gave her anything. She thought if she died, she would die of hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- when they war finished, did you think about going back to your hometown? To Grodek?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, when the war finished, Grodek wasn't my home. I was on the border, but my home was on [INAUDIBLE] in Lwow. I remember, I had-- it's written here, you have your certificate, whatever you studied. I said, yes. If I obtained, yes, but I haven't got it. Why? When the Germans came in and started, I thought of it. For the time being at the beginning, I had it with me, and guarded it all time. But if the Russians came, I had it. That's why they took me to work in a laboratorium. But later, with the Germans, it was a talk of going to ghetto. And I thought, I still didn't have the assumed name, still under my name. Even to ghetto, I don't want to keep it with me. I'm afraid I will lose it or they'll take it away or whatever. And I remember we lived on the second floor, and there was like a door from our place to the-- you know, it's different here. The houses are built that you can go in under the roof. There was a space under the roof--

speaker: interviewer
text: An attic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Attic, yes. And you could go from the side on a ladder. I went up there and took my certificate, and between-- on the roof-- between-- on the, on the-- under the roof, I hid it there. I didn't know that-- what-- never knew-- people didn't know that they will kill in concentration camps or even ghettos. I knew that I have to go down. So I hid it there. And I thought, I'll come back and get it out. Who knows if it's not still there so many years. And I hid it between there, and there it was. I never went back there. People hid a lot of things, and they never came back there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did  you try and go--

speaker: interviewee
text: So to go-- first of all, Lwow is Russian. I couldn't go-- could never go back. I was back in the beginning, as I told you the story, you know. But otherwise, after the war, I wouldn't go back. I don't think that it was safe to go back to Russia. It was very-- it wasn't safe. And to Poland, I had no one. I had some relatives in Poland. But on, on-- in [INAUDIBLE], we had cousins. But who knows if they were alive at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you try going back to Lwow after the war at all? Did you try going back to your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't get there. How did I try? It's impossible. And even so, I have to have witnesses. I know my sister, I told you, lived not far from me. And they had a big restaurant, a big house. It was [INAUDIBLE] house. I had a flat, not the whole house. It wasn't a house. It was in a house, flat. Four flats on.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they had a big house and a restaurant. And the caretaker took away all her furniture, took out everything. Now I would recognize her, and she would recognize me. But I wouldn't go to Poland. It's not worthwhile. What for?

speaker: interviewee
text: People-- you see, there was-- it is now Ukraine. They said-- and I know people that they went back to Poland if they had some houses or business, whatever. And some, they could get back in Poland. But not in Ukraine. Ukraine wouldn't give back-- There was a big mill, I told you, a flour mill. It belonged to my husband's parents and grandparents. It was theirs from generations, a big mill. They were rather quite wealthy, in two houses on the same place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But Russia would-- I can't claim it. You can-- in Poland, you can claim. But not-- not-- it's Ukraine. Ukraine is now independent. Lwow doesn't belong to Poland anymore. So I wouldn't go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time you spent in Germany after the war, which DP camps were you in? Which were the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Which DP camps? Two. First we arrived in Bamberg. That was very first-- but that was only to segregate the people to send them to different places. Because there were thousands and thousands of people. But the real first where I settled for a while and worked was Furth. Furth, near Nuremberg. In Furth, I got back my daughter. And shortly afterwards, when it was closed-- they closed slowly. They closed one by one of those DP camps because people went-- migrated from there. So the next was Feldafing. And Feldafing was more near Muenchen [INAUDIBLE]. So in two I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions like in those camps? What were the living conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camps? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Not really normal. People lived from day to day waiting to leave, to settle somewhere permanently. But it was better, much better than in [INAUDIBLE], where I was because there were [INAUDIBLE] like [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE] cities where they were smaller DP camps cramped, and people lived together in one, like the one where I found my daughter. It was a dirty place. And in one room there were two families or three families even. But in the ones that I was, no, I got only one room. But there was a stove, warm in the winter. I could cook whatever, you know, for myself and for my daughter. Clean, and it was an-- ambulance. They had everywhere with-- we had two doctors that had some certificates, Dr. Axelrod, Dr. Leitner. And-- so not-- it wasn't bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you working as a nurse all this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, those two places.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the Red Cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not anymore from the Red Cross, but from the UNRRA. They were the headquarters, UNRRA. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it wasn't too bad. At least it was clean. And you could get food more. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody lived independent in their places and could cook, you know. So it wasn't too bad. But it wasn't a normal life. We couldn't congregate in nothing like, you know, a library or-- or where we could get not a newspaper even. Nothing, no, not those [INAUDIBLE]. So people knew that's only a transit. It's for a short while, and, and everybody planned where to go. Most people went where they have some relatives. I think relatives had to send in like, an affidavit [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] requesting, you know, them. But I didn't need that. I had to go-- I could go wherever I wanted. And as I said, I never thought of Australia. But because some of-- friends-- friends that I made friends, not from my earlier life-- so we talked. And they said look, here's war and here. But I'll go to a new country far-- as far from wars. And Israel was still some, you know, wars, Yom Kippur War and this war. But to go as far from Europe as possible. We suffered so much here, so we go away forever. And that's why I went-- came to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left Germany to go to Australia, did you have any news or any idea what happened to the rest of the family at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I knew. My elder sister, I know that she gave herself up. Actually, it was like worse than committing suicide, because she didn't know how she would be killed. There was a little child of-- he wasn't four, five, a little boy-- and they took her husband. I think she was normal. She had nowhere to go, no help at all. And she told that that woman has seen her. And she told her-- she didn't even know or my name, Wanda. She knew Eda. And she said Eda is lucky, very lucky. I'm not. And she saw her going, and she told me so. She came home, the woman, the police, in the evening. He said I haven't got her anymore, your sister. I have seen that she gave herself up to a Gestapo man on the street. She said-- and she said, she was normal. She went-- came up to him and said, you took my husband. Take me and my child. So I just can't forget-- not that I can't understand it-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: She just killed her child. She gave her child-- she killed herself, all right. But she gave her child to the Gestapo. But on the other hand, she had nowhere to go. What to do? And the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The eldest brother-- that was the elder sister who lived in Israel in a kibbutz before she got married, and got married there, and came back before the war started. I think she came back about '37 or '36. I don't remember exactly the year. And she got pregnant. And my mother said, don't go back now. Have the child and then you'll go back later on. Then the war came, and she was trapped. And even with a foreign passport, you can't-- you couldn't get out. The Germans didn't recognize anything. If she's Jewish-- maybe she wouldn't be Jewish, they would let her go.  But not to Israel and not-- they didn't even try. And my brother-- she lived not far from me-- and he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] second after her, was my brother, Josef. And I loved him dearly, I think better than any other of my sisters or brothers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he, he had a young wife and first child, only a few-- I think, a few months old. And when the Soviets left and the Germans started on the other side of Poland, still not in Lwow, but there were rumors that already-- that they killed-- didn't know exactly-- but people fled-- Jewish people fled from the-- these were the Soviet [INAUDIBLE], as they called him. They told stories that first they kill all Jewish males. And if anyone can run away, should do so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother talked with his wife, and she advised him to. Most young men ran to Russia. But it's a long way. People didn't think-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they thought-- first they-- they ran away first, running on foot to [INAUDIBLE]. It was eastern Lwow, eastern Poland. It's a long way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Russians came slowly on. And they've seen a lot of people on the streets. They were all running. And they, from the air, from the planes, they shot-- started to shoot them all. And never heard of him. And he was shot. And his wife-- another thing that people were not quite normal-- took the baby of about two months. I don't even remember the name as the baby. I remember her name. She was Rosa Kimmel from [INAUDIBLE]. And they lived-- she was born in a small town near the Russian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from Lwow to the Russian border, kilo-- hundreds and hundreds of kilometers. And my her husband, my brother left running, with her blessing, of course. She took the baby in a pram and wanted to run with the baby in a pram to her-- to her parents, which was impossible. And I think they killed her, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans started to shoot, up from the air, a plane, anyone who moved on this-- on the road to Russia. They've seen it all. They lowered the airplane and shot everybody. So I'm sure they both were shot. Never heard back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the war, you were convinced that you were the only one left.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I was keen that I'm only Jewess left. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Everybody, who ever survived, thought that they were the only ones. I couldn't believe it, what I've heard, and what I-- after the prison, what I suffered in the prison and how I-- and what I have seen. So-- and those children frozen and killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought they killed everyone. I was the only one. Even-- and I didn't believe that my child survived after the war. Because in such circumstances she was left, it-- it is a miracle that she's alive, a miracle at all. Not eating, not drinking, nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: That what I want to tell you-- high on the wardrobe, high, that woman, Polish woman kept-- it was sugar-- I don't know, they don't have it here. The long, like triangles, long ones. She put it up there and kept it who knows-- for-- then she never used it. It was old dust on it, standing up there. She was always hungry, because she didn't feed her. That's what I have against her. Even if I could find her, I wouldn't look for her. Even that she was saved. She was saved, not that she wanted to save her and help her. She want to keep her for Jesus Christ. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when she left with her children for the market, well, she fed her children and didn't bring home anything for my daughter. She looked up and had seen the sugar there, and she needed sugar because she had not eaten, low blood pressure. And sugar helps, she felt it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how she got up there. I don't know-- she had a ladder. But she said-- she told me later. Said Mommy, I went up there-- maybe she had a ladder-- licked it, the sugar, a little. She came home, the woman, and had seen-- it was all dusted and when she licked it off it was white sugar underneath. She had seen it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And said, did you touch that sugar? And she was afraid to lie. She said yes, I was hungry. She hit her a few times. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Said, I didn't do it anymore. Then she said on the-- that the place where she was, it was a big square-- We'll continue next tape, sorry. Thank you. End of this tape.
